title: X
html:
X

вАЬA regular technique,вАЭ Spandrell repeated. вАЬOne chooses them unhappy, or dissatisfied, or wanting to go on the stage, or trying to write for the magazines and being rejected and consequently thinking theyвАЩre √Ґmes incomprises.вАЭ He was boastfully generalizing from the case of poor little Harriet Watkins. If he had just badly recounted his affair with Harriet it wouldnвАЩt have sounded such a very grand exploit. Harriet was such a pathetic, helpless little creature; anybody could have done her down. But generalized like this, as though her case was only one of hundreds, told in the language of the cookery book (вАЬone chooses them unhappyвАЭпїњвАФit was one of Mrs.¬†BeetonвАЩs recipes), the history sounded, he thought, most cynically impressive. вАЬAnd one starts by being very, very kind, and so wise, and perfectly pure, an elder brother, in fact. And they think oneвАЩs really wonderful, because, of course, theyвАЩve never met anybody who wasnвАЩt just a city man, with city ideas and city ambitions. Simply wonderful, because one knows all about art and has met all the celebrities and doesnвАЩt think exclusively about money and in terms of the morning paper.

вАЬAnd theyвАЩre a little in awe of one, too,вАЭ he added, remembering little HarrietвАЩs expression of scared admiration. вАЬOneвАЩs so unrespectable and yet so high-class, so at ease and at home among the great works and the great men, so wicked but so extraordinarily good, so learned, so well travelled, so brilliantly cosmopolitan and West-End (have you ever heard a suburban talking of the West-End?), like that gentleman with the order of the Golden Fleece in the advertisements for De¬†Rezske cigarettes. Yes, theyвАЩre in awe of one; but at the same time they adore. OneвАЩs so understanding, one knows so much about life in general and their souls in particular, and one isnвАЩt a bit flirtatious or saucy like ordinary men, not a bit. They feel they could trust one absolutely; and so they can, for the first weeks. One has to get them used to the trap; quite tame and trusting, trained not to shy at an occasional brotherly pat on the back or an occasional chaste uncle-ish kiss on the forehead. And meanwhile one coaxes out their little confidences, one makes them talk about love, one talks about it oneself in a man-to-man sort of way, as though they were oneвАЩs own age and as sadly disillusioned and bitterly knowing as oneselfпїњвАФwhich they find terribly shocking (though of course they donвАЩt say so), but oh, so thrilling, so enormously flattering. They simply love you for that. Well then, finally, when the moment seems ripe and theyвАЩre thoroughly domesticated and no more frightened, one stages the denouement. Tea in oneвАЩs roomsпїњвАФoneвАЩs got them thoroughly used to coming with absolute impunity to oneвАЩs roomsпїњвАФand theyвАЩre going to go out to dinner with one, so that thereвАЩs no hurry. The twilight deepens, one talks disillusionedly and yet feelingly about the amorous mysteries, one produces cocktailsпїњвАФvery strongпїњвАФand goes on talking so that they ingurgitate them absentmindedly without reflection. And sitting on the floor at their feet, one begins very gently stroking their ankles in an entirely platonic way, still talking about amorous philosophy, as though one were quite unconscious of what oneвАЩs hand were doing. If thatвАЩs not resented and the cocktails have done their work, the rest shouldnвАЩt be difficult. So, at least, IвАЩve always found.вАЭ

Spandrell helped himself to more brandy and drank. вАЬBut itвАЩs then, when theyвАЩve become oneвАЩs mistress that the fun really begins. ItвАЩs then one deploys all oneвАЩs Socratic talents. One develops their little temperaments, one domesticates themпїњвАФstill so wisely and sweetly and patientlyпїњвАФto every outrage of sensuality. It can be done, you know; the more easily, the more innocent they are. They can be brought in perfect ingenuousness to the most astonishing pitch of depravity.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩve no doubt they can,вАЭ said Mary indignantly. вАЬBut whatвАЩs the point of doing it?вАЭ

вАЬItвАЩs an amusement,вАЭ said Spandrell with theatrical cynicism. вАЬIt passes the time and relieves the tedium.вАЭ

вАЬAnd above all,вАЭ Mark Rampion went on, without looking up from his coffee cup, вАЬabove all, itвАЩs a vengeance. ItвАЩs a way of getting oneвАЩs own back on women, itвАЩs a way of punishing them for being women and so attractive, itвАЩs a way of expressing oneвАЩs hatred of them and of what they represent, itвАЩs a way of expressing oneвАЩs hatred of oneself. The trouble with you, Spandrell,вАЭ he went on, suddenly and accusingly raising his bright pale eyes to the otherвАЩs face, вАЬis that you really hate yourself. You hate the very source of your life, its ultimate basisпїњвАФfor thereвАЩs no denying it, sex is fundamental. And you hate it, hate it.вАЭ

вАЬMe?вАЭ It was a novel accusation. Spandrell was accustomed to hearing himself blamed for his excessive love of women and the sensual pleasures.

вАЬNot only you. All these people.вАЭ With a jerk of his head he indicated the other diners. вАЬAnd all the respectable ones, too. Practically everyone. ItвАЩs the disease of modern man. I call it JesusвАЩs disease on the analogy of BrightвАЩs disease. Or rather JesusвАЩs and NewtonвАЩs disease; for the scientists are as much responsible as the Christians. So are the big business men, for that matter. ItвАЩs JesusвАЩs and NewtonвАЩs and Henry FordвАЩs disease. Between them, the three have pretty well killed us. Ripped the life out of our bodies and stuffed us with hatred.вАЭ

Rampion was full of his subject. He had been busy all day on a drawing that symbolically illustrated it. Jesus, in the loincloth of the execution morning, and an overalled surgeon were represented, scalpel in hand, one on either side of an operating table, on which, foreshortened, the soles of his feet presented to the spectator, lay crucified a half-dissected man. From the horrible wound in his belly escaped a coil of entrails which, falling to the earth, mingled with those of the gashed and bleeding woman lying in the foreground, to be transformed by an allegorical metamorphosis into a whole people of living snakes. In the background receded a landscape of hills, dotted with black collieries and chimneys. On one side of the picture, behind the figure of Jesus, two angelsпїњвАФthe spiritual product of the vivisectorsвАЩ mutilationsпїњвАФwere trying to rise on their outspread wings. Vainly, for their feet were entangled in the coils of the serpents. For all their efforts, they could not leave the earth.

вАЬJesus and the scientists are vivisecting us,вАЭ he went on, thinking of his picture; вАЬhacking our bodies to bits.вАЭ

вАЬBut after all, why not?вАЭ objected Spandrell. вАЬPerhaps theyвАЩre meant to be vivisected. The fact of shame is significant. We feel spontaneously ashamed of the body and its activities. ThatвАЩs a sign of the bodyвАЩs absolute and natural inferiority.вАЭ

вАЬAbsolute and natural rubbish!вАЭ said Rampion indignantly. вАЬShame isnвАЩt spontaneous, to begin with. ItвАЩs artificial, itвАЩs acquired. You can make people ashamed of anything. Agonizingly ashamed of wearing brown boots with a black coat, or speaking with the wrong sort of accent, or having a drop at the end of their noses. Of absolutely anything, including the body and its functions. But that particular shameвАЩs just as artificial as any other. The Christians invented it, just as the tailors in Savile Row invented the shame of wearing brown boots with a black coat. There was precious little of it before Christian times. Look at the Greeks, the Etruscans.вАЭ

The antique names transported Mary back to the moors above Stanton. He was just the same. Stronger now, that was all. How ill he had looked that day! She had felt ashamed of being healthy and rich. Had she loved him then as much as she loved him now?

Spandrell had lifted a long and bony hand. вАЬI know, I know. Noble and nude and antique. But I believe theyвАЩre entirely a modern invention, those Swedish-drill pagans of ours. We trot them out whenever we want to bait the Christians. But did they ever exist? I have my doubts.вАЭ

вАЬBut look at their art,вАЭ put in Mary, thinking of the paintings at Tarquinia. She had seen them a second time with MarkпїњвАФreally seen them on that occasion.

вАЬYes, and look at ours,вАЭ retorted Spandrell. вАЬWhen the Royal Academy sculpture room is dug up three thousand years hence theyвАЩll say that Twentieth Century Londoners wore fig leaves, suckled their babies in public, and embraced one another in the parks, stark naked.вАЭ

вАЬI only wish they did,вАЭ said Rampion.

вАЬBut they donвАЩt. And thenпїњвАФleaving this question of shame on one side for the momentпїњвАФwhat about asceticism as the preliminary condition of the mystical experience?вАЭ

Rampion brought his hands together with a clap and, leaning back in his chair, turned up his eyes. вАЬOh, my sacred aunt!вАЭ he said. вАЬSo itвАЩs come to that, has it? Mystical experience and asceticism. The fornicatorвАЩs hatred of life in a new form.вАЭ

вАЬBut seriouslyпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ the other began.

вАЬNo, seriously, have you read Anatole FranceвАЩs Tha√ѓs?вАЭ

Spandrell shook his head.

вАЬRead it,вАЭ said Rampion. вАЬRead it. ItвАЩs elementary, of course. A boysвАЩ book. But one mustnвАЩt grow up without having read all the boysвАЩ books. Read it and then come and talk to me again about asceticism and mystical experiences.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩll read it,вАЭ said Spandrell. вАЬMeanwhile, all I wanted to say is that there are certain states of consciousness known to ascetics that are unknown to people who arenвАЩt ascetics.вАЭ

вАЬNo doubt. And if you treat your body in the way nature meant you to, as an equal, you attain to states of consciousness unknown to the vivisecting ascetics.вАЭ

вАЬBut the states of the vivisectors are better than the states of the indulgers.вАЭ

вАЬIn other words, lunatics are better than sane men. Which I deny. The sane, harmonious, Greek man gets as much as he can of both sets of states. HeвАЩs not such a fool as to want to kill part of himself. He strikes a balance. It isnвАЩt easy of course; itвАЩs even damnably difficult. The forces to be reconciled are intrinsically hostile. The conscious soul resents the activities of the unconscious, physical, instinctive part of the total being. The life of the one is the otherвАЩs death and vice versa. But the sane man at least tries to strike a balance. The Christians, who werenвАЩt sane, told people that theyвАЩd got to throw half of themselves in the wastepaper basket. And now the scientists and business men come and tell us that we must throw away half of what the Christians left us. But I donвАЩt want to be three quarters dead. I prefer to be alive, entirely alive. ItвАЩs time there was a revolt in favour of life and wholeness.вАЭ

вАЬBut from your point of view,вАЭ said Spandrell, вАЬI should have thought this epoch needed no reforming. ItвАЩs the golden age of guzzling, sport, and promiscuous lovemaking.вАЭ

вАЬBut if you knew what a puritan Mark really was!вАЭ Mary Rampion laughed. вАЬWhat a regular old puritan!вАЭ

вАЬNot a puritan,вАЭ said her husband. вАЬMerely sane. YouвАЩre like everyone else,вАЭ he went on, addressing himself to Spandrell. вАЬYou seem to imagine that the cold, modern, civilized lasciviousness is the same as the healthyпїњвАФwhat shall I call it?пїњвАФphallism (that gives the religious quality of the old way of life; youвАЩve read the Acharnians?)пїњвАФphallism, then, of the ancients.вАЭ

Spandrell groaned and shook his head. вАЬSpare us the Swedish exercises.вАЭ

вАЬBut it isnвАЩt the same,вАЭ the other went on. вАЬItвАЩs just Christianity turned inside out. The ascetic contempt for the body expressed in a different way. Contempt and hatred. That was what I was saying just now. You hate yourselves, you hate life. Your only alternatives are promiscuity or asceticism. Two forms of death. Why, the Christians themselves understood phallism a great deal better than this godless generation. WhatвАЩs that phrase in the marriage service? вАШWith my body I thee worship.вАЩ Worshipping with the bodyпїњвАФthatвАЩs the genuine phallism. And if you imagine it has anything to do with the unimpassioned civilized promiscuity of our advanced young people, youвАЩre very much mistaken indeed.вАЭ

вАЬOh, IвАЩm quite ready to admit the deathliness of our civilized entertainments,вАЭ Spandrell answered. вАЬThereвАЩs a certain smell,вАЭ he went on speaking in snatches between sucks at the half-smoked cigar he was trying to relight, вАЬof cheap scentпїњвАКпїњвА¶ and stale unwashednessпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I often thinkпїњвАКпїњвА¶ the atmosphere of hellпїњвАКпїњвА¶ must be composed of it.вАЭ He threw the match away. вАЬBut the other alternativeпїњвАФthereвАЩs surely no death about that. No death in Jesus or St.¬†Francis, for example.вАЭ

вАЬIn spots,вАЭ said Rampion. вАЬThey were dead in spots. Very much alive in others, I quite agree. But they simply left half of existence out of account. No, no, they wonвАЩt do. ItвАЩs time people stopped talking about them. IвАЩm tired of Jesus and Francis, terribly tired of them.вАЭ

вАЬWell then, the poets,вАЭ said Spandrell. вАЬYou canвАЩt say that ShelleyвАЩs a corpse.вАЭ

вАЬShelley?вАЭ exclaimed Rampion. вАЬDonвАЩt talk to me of Shelley.вАЭ He shook his head emphatically. вАЬNo, no. ThereвАЩs something very dreadful about Shelley. Not human, not a man. A mixture between a fairy and a white slug.вАЭ

вАЬCome, come,вАЭ Spandrell protested.

вАЬOh, exquisite and all that. But what a bloodless kind of slime inside! No blood, no real bones and bowels. Only pulp and a white juice. And oh, that dreadful lie in the soul! The way he was always pretending for the benefit of himself and everybody else that the world wasnвАЩt really the world, but either heaven or hell. And that going to bed with women wasnвАЩt really going to bed with them, but just two angels holding hands. Ugh! Think of his treatment of womenпїњвАФshocking, really shocking. The women loved it, of courseпїњвАФfor a little. It made them feel so spiritualпїњвАФthat is, until it made them feel like committing suicide. So spiritual. And all the time he was just a young schoolboy with a sensual itch like anybody elseвАЩs, but persuading himself and other people that he was Dante and Beatrice rolled into one, only much more so. Dreadful, dreadful! The only excuse is that, I suppose, he couldnвАЩt help it. He wasnвАЩt born a man; he was only a kind of fairy slug with the sexual appetites of a schoolboy. And then, think of that awful incapacity to call a spade a spade. He always had to pretend it was an angelвАЩs harp or a platonic imagination. Do you remember the ode вАШTo a SkylarkвАЩ? вАШHail to thee, blithe spirit! Bird thou never wert!вАЩвАКвАЭ Rampion recited with ludicrous parody of an elocutionistвАЩs вАЬexpression.вАЭ вАЬJust pretending, just lying to himself, as usual. The lark couldnвАЩt be allowed to be a mere bird, with blood and feathers and a nest and an appetite for caterpillars. Oh no! That wasnвАЩt nearly poetical enough, that was much too coarse. It had to be a disembodied spirit. Bloodless, boneless. A kind of ethereal flying slug. It was only to be expected. Shelley was a kind of flying slug himself; and, after all, nobody can really write about anything except himself. If youвАЩre a slug, you must write about slugs, even though your subject is supposed to be a skylark. But I wish to God,вАЭ Rampion added, with a sudden burst of comically extravagant fury, вАЬI wish to God the bird had had as much sense as those sparrows in the book of Tobit and dropped a good large mess in his eye. It would have served him damned well right for saying it wasnвАЩt a bird. Blithe spirit, indeed! Blithe spirit!вАЭ
